transcript:
Look at this swirling, smoke-like field — thousands of pale threads curling into vortex shapes on a near-black ground, brighter where they braid together, faint and ghostly where they drift alone. It reads like fog caught mid-motion, or a fingerprint stretched across a canvas.

Here's the thing: nothing in this code ever sees that swirl. In the function called "what this point knows," each location only gets a single number — a direction, built from four layered noise scales — and that's the entirety of its knowledge. Then in "trace the journey of," an agent just walks forward step by step, cosine and sine, following only the direction under its feet. It never checks where it's been or where the vortex centers are. Eighteen hundred of these blind walks, plus a second layer of two thousand five hundred short dashes sampling that same hidden field — and the vortices you're seeing are just... math nobody computed on purpose. They're what the local rule looks like from far enough away.

So when you scan this whole image in one glance, you're doing something no single line of this code can do — holding the pattern that only exists in aggregate.

of its knowledge. Then in "trace the journey of," an agent just walks forward
